_README.whatIsThis: This file is the app's KNOWLEDGE BASE of dream-psychology frameworks. It is data, not code. The app reads this file, finds entries relevant to a user's dream, and hands them (with their sources) to an LLM to phrase a grounded interpretation.
_README.howToEditSafely: Add or change entries inside the 'frameworks' list below. Each entry is wrapped in { }. Keep the commas between entries, and keep every label and text value in "double quotes". You never need to touch the app's code to update this knowledge.
_README.honestyRule: Dream interpretation is NOT settled science. Every entry is a SOURCED PERSPECTIVE, never a claim of fact. Interpretations must be attributed to the framework they come from.

frameworks.name: Jungian (Analytical Psychology)
frameworks.coreIdea: Dreams are natural, purposeful expressions of the unconscious that compensate for one-sided conscious attitudes and guide psychological growth (individuation). They speak in symbols and archetypes drawn from both personal experience and a shared 'collective unconscious'.
frameworks.howItReadsDreams: A symbol's meaning depends on the dreamer's personal associations (amplification), but recurring motifs may reflect universal archetypes (the Shadow, Anima/Animus, the Self, the Wise Old Man, the Great Mother). Dreams are seen as compensatory: they often surface what the waking mind neglects or represses.
strengths: Treats dreams as meaningful and growth-oriented rather than purely pathological
Flexible and personal; avoids a fixed symbol dictionary
Culturally rich, integrates myth and symbolism
criticisms: Concepts like the collective unconscious are difficult to test empirically
Limited support from modern cognitive neuroscience
sources: Jung, C. G. (1964). Man and His Symbols. Aldus Books.
Jung, C. G. (1974). Dreams (collected works). Princeton University Press.

frameworks.name: Freudian (Psychoanalytic)
frameworks.coreIdea: Dreams are disguised fulfillments of repressed wishes, often rooted in childhood and sexuality. They are 'the royal road to the unconscious.'
frameworks.howItReadsDreams: Distinguishes the 'manifest content' (the remembered dream) from the 'latent content' (the hidden wish). Dream-work mechanisms — condensation, displacement, symbolization, secondary revision — disguise the latent wish so it can slip past the mind's censor. Interpretation works backward via free association.
strengths: Historically foundational; introduced the idea that dreams carry psychological meaning
criticisms: Heavy emphasis on sexuality is widely seen as overstated
Modern dream science finds many dreams reflect waking concerns rather than disguised wishes
sources: Freud, S. (1900). The Interpretation of Dreams. Franz Deuticke.

frameworks.name: Hall & Van de Castle (Content Analysis)
frameworks.founder: Calvin S. Hall and Robert Van de Castle
frameworks.coreIdea: Dreams can be studied empirically by systematically coding their content (characters, social interactions, emotions, settings, objects) across large samples, revealing statistical norms and patterns rather than hidden symbols.
frameworks.howItReadsDreams: Rather than decoding symbols, this approach quantifies what actually appears in dreams. It produced normative data (e.g., dreams contain more aggression than friendliness, more misfortune than good fortune) and lets a person's dreams be compared to population norms.
keyConcepts: Continuity between dreams and waking thought
strengths: Foundation for modern quantitative dream research
criticisms: Describes patterns but offers limited 'meaning' for an individual dream
Coding can be labor-intensive and somewhat subjective
sources: Hall, C. S., & Van de Castle, R. L. (1966). The Content Analysis of Dreams. Appleton-Century-Crofts.
Hall, C. S. (1953). A cognitive theory of dreams. Journal of General Psychology.

frameworks.name: Continuity Hypothesis (Domhoff)
frameworks.coreIdea: Dreams are a dramatized, embodied expression of a person's waking conceptions and emotional concerns. There is strong continuity between what occupies us awake and what we dream about.
frameworks.howItReadsDreams: A dream is read as a window into the dreamer's ongoing preoccupations, relationships, and emotional life — not as coded symbols. If you dream about an exam, the relevant question is what in waking life feels like being tested, evaluated, or unprepared.
keyConcepts: Dreams as 'embodied simulations' of waking concerns
Consistency of dream content over time
strengths: Strong empirical backing from large dream databases
Grounded in neuroscience and cognitive psychology
Practical: ties dreams to real waking-life concerns
criticisms: Less concerned with the deeper symbolic 'meaning' some users seek
Continuity is a general pattern, not a precise decoding method
sources: Domhoff, G. W. (2003). The Scientific Study of Dreams. American Psychological Association.
Domhoff, G. W. (1996). Finding Meaning in Dreams: A Quantitative Approach. Plenum Press.

frameworks.name: Threat-Simulation Theory (Revonsuo)
frameworks.coreIdea: Dreaming is an evolved biological function: a virtual rehearsal of threatening events that improved ancestral survival by letting us practice threat recognition and avoidance in a safe, offline simulation.
frameworks.howItReadsDreams: Explains why dreams are disproportionately negative and full of chases, dangers, and misfortune. A nightmare about being chased is read as the brain running a threat-avoidance simulation, not as a coded symbol. A later extension (social simulation theory) applies the same logic to rehearsing social bonds.
strengths: Explains the prevalence of negative/threatening dream content
Consistent with trauma-related increases in threatening dreams
criticisms: Not all dreams are threatening; many are mundane or positive
Hard to prove the evolutionary 'why' directly
sources: Revonsuo, A. (2000). The reinterpretation of dreams: An evolutionary hypothesis of the function of dreaming. Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 23(6).
Revonsuo, A., Tuominen, J., & Valli, K. (2015). The avatars in the machine (social simulation theory).

frameworks.name: Modern Sleep & Neuroscience
frameworks.coreIdea: Dreaming is tied to brain activity during sleep (especially REM) and serves functions like memory consolidation and emotional processing. Some content is the cortex making sense of semi-random brainstem signals.
frameworks.howItReadsDreams: Two influential strands: (1) Activation-synthesis (Hobson & McCarley, 1977) — dreams arise as the forebrain synthesizes random REM-generated signals into a narrative, so not every detail is 'meaningful.' (2) Memory & emotion processing — sleep consolidates memories and helps defuse the emotional charge of experiences, so dreams may reflect that overnight processing.
strengths: Grounded in measurable brain activity and experiments
Explains the bizarre, fragmented quality of many dreams
Connects dreaming to clear functions (memory, emotion)
criticisms: Reduces some 'meaning' to neurobiology, which not everyone finds satisfying
Dreaming also occurs outside REM, complicating simple models
sources: Hobson, J. A., & McCarley, R. W. (1977). The brain as a dream state generator: an activation-synthesis hypothesis. American Journal of Psychiatry.
Stickgold, R., & Walker, M. P. (2013). Sleep-dependent memory triage. Nature Neuroscience.
Walker, M. (2017). Why We Sleep. Scribner.
